title: What Do Men Want in a Wife? Evidence from a Matchmaking App
gist: Onboarding data from 45,900 men versus 243,183 women on marriage-focused app Keeper.ai shows men want largely the same relational traits as women (values, communication, humor) but are far more specific and rigid than women about a partner's age and appearance, with striking sex asymmetries in acceptable height and age-gap preferences.

insights.claim: On Keeper.ai, a matchmaking platform built explicitly for marriage and family formation rather than casual dating, 243,183 women completed onboarding versus only 45,900 men -- a roughly 5-to-1 female-to-male ratio.
insights.speaker: Rosemary L. Hopcroft
insights.so_what: Precise user counts confirm acute gender-demand imbalance on commitment-oriented matchmaking platforms -- a supply constraint on the male side that such platforms and their matching algorithms must design around.
insights.locator: The numbers tell a revealing story right away

themes.theme: What men prioritize in a spouse

insights.claim: Men's top-10 self-reported traits wanted in a wife, ranked most to least frequent, are: values, relationship dynamic, communication style, sense of humor, intelligence, physical health, lifestyle, ambition, intimacy, and interests.
insights.speaker: Rosemary L. Hopcroft
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete rank-ordered list of male stated priorities that mirrors women's list closely, undercutting a purely looks-driven narrative of male mate preference.

insights.claim: Men place less emphasis on a sense of humor than women do (17.7% of men flag it as important versus 28% of women), while men are somewhat more likely than women to mention physical health (15.6% vs 11.2%) and lifestyle (15.3% vs 11.3%).
insights.speaker: Rosemary L. Hopcroft
insights.so_what: Quantifies specific sex gaps in trait weighting beyond the shared top-line list, useful for calibrating matching-algorithm weights by gender.
insights.locator: Men, it seems, are less concerned

insights.claim: Nearly 45% of men on Keeper report they would accept a wife who is taller than they are, whereas only 10.2% of women say they would accept a husband who is shorter than they are -- a stark sex asymmetry in height flexibility.
insights.speaker: Rosemary L. Hopcroft
insights.so_what: Height flexibility is heavily concentrated on the male side, meaning taller women and shorter men face a materially tighter matching pool on height-based platforms.
insights.quote: Nearly 45% of men using the Keeper app report that they would accept a wife who is taller than they are, whereas only 10.2%  of women say they would accept a husband who is shorter
insights.locator: Responses concerning height reveal a striking sex difference

insights.claim: Men's preferred age gap widens with their own age: the youngest men (18-21) prefer slightly older partners, but starting at ages 22-25 men prefer progressively younger partners, with the median preferred gap growing from about 1.5 years younger at 22-25 to about 10 years younger for men 50 and older.
insights.speaker: Rosemary L. Hopcroft
insights.so_what: The age-gap preference compounds with male age rather than staying constant -- a pattern directly opposite to women's narrowing-gap pattern, and a key input for modeling remarriage/dating-market dynamics for older men.
insights.locator: The median preferred age gap ranges from approximately

insights.claim: Men are roughly 11 times more likely than women to classify a partner's age as very important or non-negotiable (16.1% of men versus 1.4% of women).
insights.speaker: Rosemary L. Hopcroft
insights.so_what: Age rigidity is a heavily male-skewed screening criterion, useful for predicting which users will filter matches most aggressively by age on a platform.
insights.locator: men are approximately 11 times more likely than women

insights.claim: Men are generally less specific than women across most preference categories, but are more than five times as likely as women to flag body figure as an important appearance trait (8.2% vs 1.5%), and are more likely to unwillingly relocate (26.7% vs 14.0% of women).
insights.speaker: Rosemary L. Hopcroft
insights.so_what: Body-figure specificity is the clear exception to men's general vagueness about appearance -- a concrete, non-negotiable filter for a meaningful minority of men.
insights.locator: men are more than five times as likely as women

insights.claim: Among men who list undesirable spousal traits (25.9% of men, versus 30.6% of women), 'overweight' is the most frequently cited unwanted trait (8.7% of men vs 5.8% of women), and 'ugly' ranks third for men -- a trait that does not appear on women's top-10 unwanted list at all.
insights.speaker: Rosemary L. Hopcroft
insights.so_what: Weight and appearance dealbreakers are disproportionately salient for men, giving matchmaking products a concrete negative-filter signal specific to male users.
insights.locator: What Men on Keeper Don't Want

insights.claim: The author attributes men's stronger stated preference for youth and beauty to evolutionary psychology: younger, more attractive partners historically signaled greater reproductive potential, so men who preferred such traits would, on average, have left more descendants.
insights.speaker: Rosemary L. Hopcroft
insights.so_what: Provides the theoretical lens the author uses throughout to interpret the Keeper data -- useful for gauging how much of the analysis is descriptive statistics versus adaptationist interpretation.
insights.locator: The evolutionary rationale for the preference for younger partners

insights.claim: The author cautions that this Keeper data reflects stated preferences, not actual mate choices, and that real marriages are typically a compromise between what each partner says they want.
insights.speaker: Rosemary L. Hopcroft
insights.so_what: A methodological caveat that should temper any product or investment thesis built purely on stated-preference dating-app data -- revealed choices will diverge from these figures.
insights.quote: this Keeper data is derived from what men say they want in a long-term partner
insights.locator: Of course, this Keeper data is derived
